name: Open Source
description: Community-driven, inclusive, well-documented. Prioritize contributor experience, avoid exclusionary patterns, default to permissive choices.

extras.text: Is this workaround common in the community?
Can a new contributor understand and modify this within 30 minutes of reading?
What does a thriving, active contributor community look like for this project in 12 months? What would make someone want to contribute vs. just use it?
Does this change affect the contributor path (CONTRIBUTING.md, dev setup, CI, issue templates)? If yes, update it in the same task.

specSections.title: Contributor Guide
specSections.placeholder: _Describe the contributor pathway: forking, dev environment setup, submitting a PR. What is the Time-To-First-Contribution? List every manual step._

specSections.title: Public API Surface
specSections.placeholder: _Map every public export, CLI flag, and config option touched by this change. Are names self-explanatory without docs? Any breaking changes?_

specSections.title: Release Notes
specSections.placeholder: _Draft the release notes for this change. Audience: users upgrading from the previous version. Focus on what changes for them, not on implementation details._

reminders: Endpoint should be documented in API docs
Consider contributor experience for new patterns
New features and API changes must be documented so contributors can discover and use them
OSS Prime Directive: a first-time contributor should be able to fork, modify, and submit a PR within 30 minutes. Track your setup steps — every undocumented step is a contributor you lose.
OSS Prime Directive: CHANGELOG must be updated with every user-facing change. Contributors need to know what changed between releases.
OSS Prime Directive: Default to the simplest thing that works. Clever code is a barrier; readable code is a welcome mat.
Permissive licenses (MIT, Apache 2.0) by default. Any copyleft dependency needs explicit justification and legal review.
acceptanceCriteria: Changes reflected in README or relevant docs
CHANGELOG updated for any user-facing change
CONTRIBUTING.md exists and is accurate after this change
CI pipeline passes — external contributors can fork and get green CI without manual intervention
No new hard-coded environment assumptions (absolute paths, machine-specific config) that would break on a contributor's machine

reviewDimensions.label: Contributor Onboarding
reviewDimensions.prompt: Walk through the contributor journey: clone → install → run → modify → test → PR. Where does it break? What's undocumented? What would confuse someone seeing this repo for the first time?

reviewDimensions.label: API Surface Quality
reviewDimensions.prompt: Review every public export, CLI flag, and config option touched by this change. Are names self-explanatory? Are defaults sensible? Is progressive disclosure working (simple things simple, complex things possible)?

reviewDimensions.label: Documentation Findability
reviewDimensions.prompt: For each new feature: can a user discover it from README? Are code examples copy-paste runnable? Is there a gap between 'documented' and 'findable'?

reviewDimensions.label: Contributor Friction Score
reviewDimensions.prompt: Count the number of manual steps a new contributor must take from `git clone` to `first successful test run`. Score: 0-2 steps = excellent, 3-5 = acceptable, 6+ = blocking. List every step, flag every friction point. Are setup prerequisites documented? Are error messages helpful when setup fails?

reviewDimensions.label: License Consistency
reviewDimensions.prompt: Check every new dependency introduced by this change: is its license compatible with this project's license? Any copyleft (GPL, LGPL, AGPL) license in a library used by an MIT project is a legal landmine. List every new dependency, its license, and compatibility verdict.

dreamStatePrompt: In 12 months, what does a healthy contributor community look like for this codebase? New contributors submit first PRs within a day. Existing contributors can find their way around any module. Issues have clear reproduction steps. PRs get reviewed in 48 hours. CHANGELOG reads like a product changelog, not a commit log.
